explicit_text: I remember when people in my neighborhood talked about going to America, calling it the land of plenty. When I was a child, around the 1920s, everyone seemed to think America was a place where money grew on trees. I arrived in New York City first and quickly realized it wasn't quite like the stories I had heard. The United States was this big, exciting place full of hope, but also full of surprises.

explicit_text: When I told people I was going to Chicago, they reacted with all sorts of comments, especially about the dangers there. I was only nine years old in the 1920s, and someone even made gunshot sounds, 'bang, bang, bang,' referring to Al Capone and the gangsters of that era. Chicago was this mysterious and somewhat frightening place in the stories I heard, but it was also my destination after arriving in New York. The city was a big part of my journey to America.

explicit_text: As a child in the 1920s, I didn't understand much about Al Capone, but people around me talked about him a lot when I mentioned going to Chicago. Someone made 'bang, bang, bang' noises, which I later learned referred to the violence associated with Capone's gang. At the time, I was just nine years old and found it all quite funny, not realizing the seriousness behind those stories. Al Capone was part of the backdrop of Chicago during my early years of immigration.

explicit_text: When I first arrived in America, I landed in New York City and was struck by how different it was from the stories I had heard. People told me money grew on trees in America, but in New York, I didn't see any dollar bills hanging from the trees. From there, I took a train to Chicago, checking every tree along the way, hoping to find some money. New York City was my first impression of the United States, a bustling gateway full of hope and reality.
